title: Yossi Matias on the golden age of research
summary: Yossi Matias, VP and Head of Google Research, discusses Google's research mission and what he calls a "golden age" of scientific research enabled by AI. The conversation covers applications in flood forecasting, medical AI with MedGemma, AI tools for scientific discovery including Co-Scientist and ERA, and emerging capabilities like speculative decoding and generative UI.
writeup: A conversation between Logan Kilpatrick (Google DeepMind) and Yossi Matias (Google Research) covering the breadth and mission of Google Research across planetary intelligence, health AI, weather modeling, and foundational ML. The through-line is Matias's "magic cycle of research": breakthrough work that lands in products and creates the next open question, now accelerating with AI. Concrete examples anchor every claim, from MedGemma's 5 million downloads to NeuralGCM delivering flood and storm warnings to 38 million farmers in India.
key_points: Google Research frames its mission as making the impossible possible through a "magic cycle" in which breakthrough research is applied to products, which generates the next research question, a loop that AI is now accelerating.

MedGemma, Google's open-weight health model released on the Gemma platform, has surpassed 5 million downloads and is being used in low-connectivity settings including offline applications in Uganda credited with saving lives.

Google Earth AI aggregates geospatial and remote sensing foundation models under an agentic layer, enabling downstream research such as a Harvard/Boston Children's Hospital/Mount Sinai Nature paper analyzing measles vaccination coverage at the zip-code level.

NeuralGCM, developed in collaboration with the University of Chicago, provided weather warnings to 38 million farmers in India, illustrating the scale at which domain-specific AI models can reach previously unserved populations.

Matias frames AI not as a replacement for human expertise but as an amplifier of human ingenuity, pointing to empowerment of healthcare workers, teachers, and scientists as the most consequential near-term frontier.

The discussion argues that foundational computer science thinking becomes more valuable, not less, as AI tools proliferate, drawing the analogy to math remaining important after calculators became a commodity.
